images.caption: Figure 1:Conformal agent error attribution isolates the decisive error in a failed MAS trajectory within a conformal prediction set, providing statistical guarantees of coverage.

Figure 3:Left filtering withFLF​(x;q)F_{\text{LF}}(x;q)progressively removes steps from the left asqqdecreases. The smallestqqwhich retains the decisive errory∗y^{*}is used as the conformal scoreSLF​(x,y∗)S_{\text{LF}}(x,y^{*}).

Figure 4:Two-way filtering withFTWF​(x;q)F_{\text{TWF}}(x;q)uses the intersection of filtering from the right and left. The smallestqqwhich retains the decisive errory∗y^{*}is the conformal scoreSTWF​(x,y∗)S_{\text{TWF}}(x,y^{*}).
Figure 5:After generating a conformal set for a failed task, we roll back the state of the MAS to the first step in the set, and restart the agent with information on the failed trace.
Figure 6:Normalized decisive error position distributions for the real-world benchmark (Who&When), and controlled error injection datasets (Left/Mid/Right Dense) on GSM8k.
Figure 7:Empirical Coverage vs. Target Coverage for CP methods with the fine-tuned LLM scoring function on MATH.

images.caption: Figure 8:Example rollback scenario with text for the task, decisive error, final answer, and corrected versions after the rollback. We first generate a conformal set for the failed task, roll back the state of the MAS to the first step in the prediction set, and restart the agent with instructions to avoid the same mistakes.
